$comment: Pinned upstream binaries shipped inside this repository under bin/.
Each entry corresponds exactly to one file under bin/. The runtime
verifies the SHA256 at startup against this manifest before exec'ing.
To update a pinned binary:
1. Bump the version + url + sha256 fields below
2. Run `make sync-bins` (downloads + verifies)
3. Commit the changes (manifest + bin/) together
CI runs `make verify-bins` on every push to ensure manifest and bin/
stay in lockstep.

$changelog_v0_1_1: Linux: --health probeTun no longer requires net-tools (`ifconfig`).
v0.1.0 shelled out to `ifconfig <tun>` and on stock Ubuntu cloud
images that produced an exec error, so checks.tun stayed false
forever and ensure-ready always reported `degraded` even when
the tunnel was perfectly healthy. We now read
/sys/class/net/<tun>/flags (kernel sysfs, dependency-free) and
fall back to `ip link show` and finally `ifconfig` for the macOS
/ net-tools-installed cases.
Linux: --health probeDefaultRoute is now PBR-aware. Router v0.1.1+
intentionally leaves the main routing table untouched, so the
old `default via tunGw` check would always say hijacked=false.
We now also inspect `ip route show default table 13579` plus the
`ip rule … priority 29870 lookup 13579` entry that the C++
router installs; either signal sets route.hijacked=true and
reports route.mode="pbr".
All platforms: --health probeDNS now returns hijacked=true when
the live resolvers diverge from the snapshot recorded in
state.json's original_dns at daemon startup. Previously it was
hard-coded to false, so callers could never tell the router had
rewritten /etc/resolv.conf.

$comment: Privileged C++ daemon that owns route + DNS mutations. Source:
feivpn-apps/client/electron/{linux,macos}_proxy_controller. The Go
daemon (feivpn) talks to it over a local socket:
Linux  → unix:/var/run/feivpn_controller
Lifecycle is managed by feivpnctl as a separate systemd unit /
LaunchDaemon; feivpn.service Wants/After feivpn-router.service.
macOS shipping model mirrors the upstream Electron client: a single
Universal Binary (lipo arm64 + x86_64). Both darwin-arm64 and
darwin-amd64 keys point at bin/feivpn-router-darwin-universal — the
SHA must match because it's the same file on disk.
$changelog_v0_1_7: Linux: stop emitting `options use-vc` in /etc/resolv.conf when
outlineDNSServer is the link-local FakeIP server (169.254/16).
This was the second half of the FakeIP-on-Linux regression that
router-v0.1.6 only partially fixed: PBR was correct, the daemon's
proxy connection no longer looped, but every glibc-based client
(curl, getent, wget, …) still hung indefinitely on DNS while
nslookup worked fine.
Root cause: outline historically wrote `options use-vc` so that
glibc's resolver would query DNS over TCP — an anti-poisoning
measure when the resolver was a real public IP (9.9.9.9) whose
TCP/53 actually answered. router-v0.1.5 swung the resolver
pointer to 169.254.113.53 (the daemon's in-process FakeIP DNS
server) but kept use-vc, breaking everything:
- nslookup uses UDP unconditionally → lwIP PacketProxy →
fakeIPSession.WriteTo intercepts port 53 → returns 198.18.X.
Works.
- glibc honours use-vc → TCP DNS to 169.254.113.53:53 → lwIP
terminates TCP → fakeip Interceptor sees a dst IP that's
NOT in 198.18.0.0/16 → falls through to the proxy dialer →
daemon happily forwards `169.254.113.53:53` over the
trojan tunnel to the egress server, which obviously cannot
route a link-local RFC 3927 address. The DNS call hangs
until the application times out. Confirmed in the daemon
log:
trojan: attempting connection target=169.254.113.53:53
server=47.107.109.223:10001 network=tcp
trojan: TCP stream established target=169.254.113.53:53
- Symptom: nslookup api.ipify.org returns 198.18.0.X correctly,
but `getent ahosts api.ipify.org` is empty and `curl
https://api.ipify.org` times out at the 'Resolving timed out'
stage.
Fix: emit use-vc only when outlineDNSServer is a public resolver
(anything outside 169.254.0.0/16). For the FakeIP topology the
in-process DNS server lives on the local link, UDP cannot be
poisoned, and use-vc just blocks the only path that actually
reaches the FakeIP server. Backwards-compatible: operators who
manually point outlineDNSServer at a public resolver still get
the legacy TCP DNS behaviour.
$changelog_v0_1_6: Linux: fix complete TCP lockout that surfaced in router-v0.1.5 once
FakeIP DNS started returning synthetic IPs for every domain.
Symptom: nslookup returned 198.18.0.X correctly but every curl /
TCP connect through the tunnel hung until timeout. The DNS half
of the loop worked because of configureDirectDNSBypass, the TCP
half didn't because the proxy server's /32 lived ONLY in the
main routing table.
Root cause: createRouteforOutlineServer() installed
'<proxyIP>/32 via <gw>' into the main table only. After
enablePolicyRouting() raised `ip rule … priority 29870 not fwmark
0x2333 lookup 13579`, every locally-originated TCP socket the Go
daemon opened to the proxy hit table 13579 first (priority 29870
< main's 32766), found `default via <tunRouter> dev <tun>`, was
routed back into the tunnel, picked up by lwIP, dialed back out
to the proxy IP again — infinite loop. The Go daemon's electron
path uses the default `transport.TCPDialer{}` with no SO_MARK,
so SO_MARK could not save us; preserveEstablishedConnections()
also explicitly excluded outlineServerIP under the (now-incorrect)
assumption that the main-table entry was sufficient.
Fix: createRouteforOutlineServer() now installs the proxy /32
bypass into BOTH the main table AND PBR table 13579 (mirrors
configureDirectDNSBypass, which got the same treatment in
router-v0.1.5). Uses `replace` so a stale entry from a crashed
instance is overwritten. Failure to install in the PBR table
is fatal — without it the tunnel literally cannot route a single
byte. deleteOutlineServerRouting() now removes the entry from
both tables, tolerating ESRCH the same way
cleanupPreservedConnections / cleanupDirectDNSBypass do.
preserveEstablishedConnections() no longer erases outlineServerIP
from the snapshot — the explicit /32 install in
createRouteforOutlineServer is the source of truth, and the
snapshot's `replace` is idempotent. The misleading 'main table
already has it' comment is rewritten to reflect PBR reality.
$changelog_v0_1_5: Linux: align DNS topology with macOS / Windows / iOS / Android by
'nameserver 169.254.113.53'. The Go daemon already runs a FakeIP
the system at it, Linux was the odd one out querying 9.9.9.9
through the tunnel. After this bump nslookup returns
198.18.0.X (synthetic) just like on Mac/Win, the proxy receives
the original hostname (ATYP=Domain), and there is no DNS
round-trip through the tunnel before each TCP connect.
Linux: directDNSServer (the optional public-resolver bypass) is
now empty by default — the FakeIP topology routes every DNS
query to 169.254.113.53, so a separate /32 bypass for
114.114.114.114 just rerouted ordinary DNS off the proxy. The
bypass machinery itself is kept in case operators want to opt
in to a split-horizon resolver, and was made PBR-correct: it
now installs the /32 in BOTH the main routing table and the
PBR table 13579 (a main-only entry was bypassed by our
priority-29870 ip rule and never matched anything).
$changelog_v0_1_4: Further router.log noise reduction. No behavioral change.
shutdown lines from WARN to INFO — these are the standard
systemd / `kill` shutdown signals, not anomalies. SIGHUP and
any other signal stay at WARN.
Demote 'tun device feivpn-tun1 already exists' line from WARN to
INFO and rewrite the message: in the feivpn-runtime topology
the Go daemon owns the TUN, so seeing it on a router restart is
the structurally expected case (the kernel keeps the device
alive while the daemon holds an FD on it).
ENOENT / 'Cannot find' branch when removing /32 bypass routes.
disablePolicyRouting() flushes the entire private table first,
so individual deletes always race against an already-empty
table. Real failures still surface as WARN.
$changelog_v0_1_3: Cosmetics + log noise reduction. No behavioral change to routing,
DNS, or connection preservation — purely a router.log readability
pass.
Banner: drop the legacy '[for OutlineVPN Client]' suffix.
Log strings: rename the user-visible 'outline server / outline
gateway / outline proxy / outline DNS server / outline tun
interface' lines to 'vpn server / vpn gateway / vpn proxy / vpn
DNS server / vpn tun interface'. Internal C++ identifiers are
unchanged so source diffs stay small.
Unix socket group: rename the lookup name from 'outlinevpn' to
'feivpn'. Demote the 'no such group on this system' message
from WARN to INFO — feivpn-runtime runs every consumer as root
via systemd, so the group's absence is the expected case and
was producing a false alarm in router.log.
Network monitor teardown: in the feivpn-runtime topology the Go
daemon disconnects the unix socket the moment configureRouting
returns. The Start() coroutine cancels the network-monitor
awaitable via boost::asio's `||` operator on every healthy
session, which used to surface as a WARN ('Operation canceled
[system:125]') after every connect. Now caught as
operation_aborted / ECANCELED and logged at INFO ('network
monitor stopped (client session ended)').
$changelog_v0_1_2: Linux: close the CONNMARK race that could still drop SSH on the
very first ensure-ready after install. v0.1.1's CONNMARK rules
only stamp a conntrack entry when an inbound packet hits INPUT,
so any outbound packet sshd emits in the few milliseconds before
the first client ACK lands would route into the tunnel and the
client would tear the connection down before recovery.
Linux: routeThroughOutline() now snapshots /proc/net/tcp at hijack
time and pre-installs `<remote>/32 via <gw> dev <eth> table 13579`
for every ESTABLISHED TCP peer. Even before any conntrack mark
exists, the very first server-originated packet to one of those
peers finds a /32 in the private table and stays on the physical
NIC. CONNMARK continues to handle new inbound connections after
cleanup paths drop the /32 entries explicitly (disablePolicyRouting
flushes the whole table anyway, but the explicit removal keeps the
$changelog_v0_1_1: Linux: switch route hijack to policy-based routing (PBR).
Old behavior (v0.1.0): delete every default route in main table
and replace with `default via 10.0.85.2 dev feivpn-tun1`. This
blackholed every existing TCP session (incl. the operator's
own SSH) the moment configureRouting was acknowledged.
New behavior: leave main table untouched. Add `default via
10.0.85.2 dev feivpn-tun1` to private table 13579 + install
`ip rule add not fwmark 0x2333 lookup 13579 priority 29870`.
The fwmark matches DirectMarkValue in the Go daemon's
direct_dialer_linux.go so the daemon's outbound trojan socket
falls through to the physical NIC.
Linux: preserve inbound TCP via iptables CONNMARK (mangle table).
INPUT  -i <eth>  -j CONNMARK --set-mark 0x2333
OUTPUT -m connmark --mark 0x2333 -j MARK --set-mark 0x2333
Reply packets for SSH / locally-hosted services keep flowing
out the physical interface even after the policy rule flips on.
Linux: only the linux-amd64 binary is rebuilt in this release;
linux-arm64 / darwin-universal still ship the v0.1.0 codepath.
They will be rebuilt the next time the build pipeline runs on
a host that has docker linux/arm64 / a macOS toolchain.
